name: Harold Arlen
abstract: Harold Arlen (born Hyman Arluck; February 15, 1905 – April 23, 1986) was an American composer of popular music, who composed over 500 songs, a number of which have become known worldwide. In addition to composing the songs for the 1939 film The Wizard of Oz (lyrics by Yip Harburg), including "Over the Rainbow", which won him the Oscar fo…

about_quotes.speaker: James Lincoln Collier
about_quotes.evidence: his dad had been a musician and played with The Buffalodians, which was the band from Buffalo that Harold Arlen had started out in.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Lincoln Collier interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Burrill Crohn
about_quotes.evidence: he did one on Harold Arlen and he did some beautiful films... Don McGlynn who did Louie Prima and Harold Arlen
about_quotes.doc_title: Burrill Crohn interviewed by Monk Rowe, Woodstock, New York, January 3, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: I was with Free and Easy, with Harold Arlen and Quincy Jones.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Lawrence Brown
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee refers to 'Harold' as a sibling ('I didn't take Harold', 'Harold was in school about three years or so behind me') and later mentions 'Harold Arlen' in the context of New York stars ('So there was Harold Arlen and Ethel Waters...'). The text does not describe a professional or person

about_quotes.speaker: Walter P. Thomas
about_quotes.evidence: Harold Arlen was a piano player with a band from Buffalo. And he had that... I don't know how to say it. He had that talent that Black musicians have. He had that Blues thing. Because that's how... It was amazing... I got an idea of repetition through hearing that.

about_quotes.speaker: Noah Evans
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Harold Arlen only in the context of a song performed by the Golden Gate Quartet and as a composer alongside Cole Porter, Rodgers and Hart, etc., with no indication of personal interaction.
